1.2.a.info:
A two-tier campus design — often called collapsed core — merges the core and distribution layers of the classic Cisco hierarchy into a single layer of switches. Those switches sit above the access layer and perform both backbone forwarding and distribution-layer policy. The access layer underneath stays unchanged: Layer 2 switches connecting endpoints.

When is it appropriate? Cisco's rule of thumb is fewer than ~200 users or a single building / small campus. If you only have 4-8 access switches, a dedicated core layer is overkill — you would pay for extra hardware that simply sits between two distribution switches and a handful of access switches. Collapsing the two layers removes cost and complexity while still giving you redundancy if you deploy the collapsed layer as a pair.

What the collapsed layer does: inter-VLAN routing (via SVIs or routed ports), ACLs and QoS policy, HSRP/VRRP/GLBP for default-gateway redundancy, route summarization toward the WAN edge, and high-speed aggregation of access uplinks. Because there is no separate core, the same pair of switches also acts as the transit fabric between distribution blocks and the WAN/edge router.

Tradeoffs: lower CapEx and simpler troubleshooting (fewer layers to walk through), but scalability is capped. Once you add more access switches than the collapsed core can comfortably aggregate, or you need to isolate failure domains across buildings, you have to migrate to a full three-tier design. Many enterprises start as collapsed core and grow into three-tier as user count climbs past a few hundred.

Exam framing: if the question mentions small campus, single building, under ~200 users, cost-sensitive, the answer is almost always two-tier / collapsed core.

hack.memory: Two-tier = two-story building. Ground floor = access (users). Top floor = combined core + distribution. No mezzanine (dedicated core) because the building is too small to justify it.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: build 2 L3 switches as collapsed core (Cat 3650/9300 equivalents) with HSRP between them. Hang 3 access switches off both L3 switches with dual uplinks. Configure SVIs for 2 VLANs. Verify 'show standby brief' and ping across VLANs.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24 (LAN Architectures). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam favors scenario questions — 'small campus, 150 users, single building' → answer: two-tier/collapsed core.

1.2.a.info:
The collapsed core/distribution switch is the single L3 switch (or pair) that performs all the functions normally split between the core and distribution layers in a three-tier design. In Cisco product terms, it is typically a Catalyst 9300, 9500, or 3850 — a multilayer switch with hardware-accelerated routing, robust ACL/QoS capabilities, and high-speed uplinks (10G/25G/40G).

Combined responsibilities:

  • Inter-VLAN routing via Switch Virtual Interfaces (interface vlan X + ip address) or routed ports (no switchport).
  • Policy enforcement — ACLs for traffic filtering, QoS marking/trust boundaries, storm control, DHCP snooping trust.
  • First-hop redundancy — HSRP or VRRP so endpoints have a virtual default gateway that survives a switch failure.
  • High-speed aggregation — bundling access-switch uplinks into EtherChannels and providing transit to the WAN edge router or firewall.
  • Spanning-tree root — usually configured as STP root bridge for all VLANs (spanning-tree vlan X root primary).

Deployed as a pair: best practice is two collapsed-core switches connected to each other (often via StackWise, VSS, or a plain L3 link) and to every access switch. Each access switch dual-homes to both collapsed-core switches. This preserves redundancy even though you have lost the dedicated core layer.

Required platform: must be an L3-capable switch. Enabling routing requires ip routing in global config — without that command, the switch is just an expensive L2 switch and SVIs will be reachable only locally.

hack.memory: Collapsed core switch = Swiss Army knife of campus networking. One box (deployed as a pair) that routes, filters, prioritizes, and failovers — everything the core+distribution would have done in a bigger design.
hack.practice: Enable 'ip routing' on a 3650/9300 in Packet Tracer. Create SVIs for VLAN 10 and 20. Configure HSRP on both switches so VLAN 10 shares a virtual IP. Shut one switch — hosts should keep reaching their gateway via the standby.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 17-18 (VLANs + Inter-VLAN Routing). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17. Exam: know that 'ip routing' is REQUIRED for SVI routing on an L3 switch — missing it is a classic exam trap.

1.2.a.info:
In a two-tier design, the access layer is functionally identical to the access layer in a three-tier design — only the layer above it changes. Access switches are the network edge, providing ports to PCs, IP phones, printers, wireless APs, cameras, and any other endpoint.

Access-layer responsibilities:

  • Port-level VLAN assignment — switchport mode access + switchport access vlan X.
  • Voice VLAN for IP phones — switchport voice vlan Y on the same port as the data VLAN.
  • PoE/PoE+ delivery for phones, APs, and cameras (15.4W / 30W per port typical).
  • 802.1X port authentication so only authorized devices get network access.
  • Port security — limits MAC addresses per port, shuts down on violation.
  • DHCP snooping + Dynamic ARP Inspection to prevent rogue DHCP servers and ARP poisoning.
  • QoS trust boundary — access switch decides whether to trust endpoint QoS markings (typically don't trust PC markings, do trust IP-phone CoS).

Typical hardware: Catalyst 2960, 9200, or 9200L — Layer 2 switches with 24 or 48 access ports plus 1-4 uplink ports. Uplinks are usually 1G or 10G fiber/copper to the collapsed core above.

Dual-homing: each access switch should uplink to both collapsed-core switches so the access layer survives the failure of one upstream switch. In STP, one uplink will be blocking; with EtherChannel or Multi-Chassis EtherChannel both can be active.

hack.memory: Access layer = the checkout counter where customers (endpoints) enter the store. You card them (802.1X), limit how many you let in (port security), check their receipts (DHCP snooping), and route them to the right aisle (VLAN).
hack.practice: On a 2960 in Packet Tracer: configure interface Gi0/1 with access VLAN 10, voice VLAN 20, port-security max 2, spanning-tree portfast, and bpduguard. Plug a PC and phone in. Verify with 'show port-security' and 'show interfaces switchport'.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17 (VLANs) + Day 50 (security fundamentals). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam: PortFast + BPDU Guard on access ports is 100% guaranteed content.

1.2.a.info:
First Hop Redundancy Protocols (FHRPs) let two (or more) routers/L3 switches share a virtual IP so that endpoints on the LAN have a default gateway that survives any single device failure. The endpoint points at the virtual IP; whichever physical device is currently active responds to ARPs and forwards traffic.

HSRP (Hot Standby Router Protocol) — Cisco proprietary. Elects one active and one standby router per group. Default hello/hold timers = 3s/10s. Uses virtual MAC 0000.0c07.acXX where XX = group number. Default priority = 100, higher wins. Enable preemption with standby X preempt to force recovery when the preferred router comes back.

VRRP (Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol) — IETF open standard (RFC 5798). Elects one master and backups. Default timers = 1s/3s, uses virtual MAC 0000.5e00.01XX. Default priority = 100; priority 255 means you own the real IP being used as the virtual IP.

GLBP (Gateway Load Balancing Protocol) — Cisco proprietary. Unlike HSRP/VRRP which are active/standby, GLBP load-balances across up to 4 gateways by handing out different virtual MACs to different clients.

Where deployed in two-tier: on the collapsed core/distribution switches, one FHRP group per VLAN/SVI. Clients receive the virtual IP as their default gateway (via DHCP option or static config). On CCNA you must know: HSRP is Cisco-only, VRRP is standard, GLBP load-balances. Configuration basics:

interface vlan 10
 ip address 10.0.10.2 255.255.255.0
 standby 10 ip 10.0.10.1
 standby 10 priority 110
 standby 10 preempt

hack.memory: HSRP = two waiters sharing one name tag. Customers (endpoints) ask for 'Bob' (virtual IP). Whichever waiter is Active wears the Bob tag and takes the order. If Active Bob trips and falls, Standby Bob grabs the tag instantly. Priority = seniority, preempt = 'let me take my tag back when I recover'.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer two-switch collapsed core. Configure HSRP group 10 on both with virtual IP 10.0.10.1. Set priority 110 + preempt on one. Ping continuously from a host, then shut the active switch's uplink — pings should recover in <10s. 'show standby brief' shows active/standby roles.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42 (FHRP). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 22. Exam: know HSRP = Cisco, VRRP = standard, GLBP = load-balancing. Know the virtual MAC format for HSRP (0000.0c07.acXX) — this is often a pure memorization question.

1.2.a.info:
Picking two-tier vs three-tier is a sizing and growth decision, not a technical-capability one. Two-tier is correct when all of the following are true:

  • User count under ~200 — the collapsed core can comfortably aggregate traffic without becoming a bottleneck.
  • Single building or tightly clustered multi-building site — cable runs are short, you don't need a dedicated core to interconnect distant distribution blocks.
  • Limited growth expected — you're not going to quadruple users in the next 2 years.
  • Cost-sensitive — you want fewer boxes, less rack space, less power, less licensing.
  • Simple traffic patterns — mostly north-south (users to internet/WAN), not heavy east-west server-to-server.

When to migrate to three-tier: once user count climbs past a few hundred, or you add additional buildings that each need their own distribution block, or when the collapsed-core pair becomes a performance or failure-domain concern. Migration is typically done by inserting a dedicated core layer above the existing distribution switches — the access layer doesn't change.

Classic exam stems: 'A 100-user law firm in a single building wants a cost-effective campus LAN' → two-tier. 'A university with 5 buildings and 3,000 users' → three-tier. 'A small startup outgrowing its flat network' → two-tier as the first step.

Design anti-pattern to avoid: using three-tier for a small network. You end up with core switches that have almost no traffic and add cost + failure points without benefit.

hack.memory: Two-tier = startup office. Three-tier = university. If you can see every user's desk from the IT closet, two-tier. If you need a golf cart to reach the other side of the campus, three-tier.
hack.practice: Draw 3 fictional orgs on paper: 50-user accountant firm, 400-user regional office, 5000-user university. Pick the architecture for each and justify in one sentence. This rehearses the exam question pattern exactly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: the trigger words are small, cost, single building → two-tier. Know why you wouldn't pick three-tier for a small site (wasted cost).

1.2.b.info:
In a three-tier design, the access layer is the Layer 2 edge where endpoints plug in. Functionally it is identical to the two-tier access layer — same duties, same hardware class, same configuration. The only difference is what sits above it: a dedicated distribution layer instead of a collapsed core.

Core access-layer responsibilities:

  • Per-port VLAN assignment (switchport mode access + switchport access vlan X), plus voice VLANs for phones.
  • Power over Ethernet — 802.3af (PoE, 15.4W), 802.3at (PoE+, 30W), 802.3bt (PoE++, 60W/90W) for phones, APs, cameras.
  • 802.1X authentication with RADIUS, optionally with dynamic VLAN assignment.
  • Port security — switchport port-security maximum N limits MACs per port; violation modes: protect / restrict / shutdown.
  • DHCP snooping + Dynamic ARP Inspection to block rogue servers and ARP poisoning at the edge.
  • STP edge features — spanning-tree portfast for fast endpoint convergence and bpduguard to shut down the port if a rogue switch is plugged in.
  • QoS trust boundary — trust markings from IP phones, rewrite markings from PCs.

Uplinks: each access switch has 1-4 uplinks, typically to both distribution switches (dual-homing) for redundancy. Common uplink speeds: 1G or 10G fiber.

Typical hardware: Catalyst 2960-X/XR, 9200, 9200L, 9300 (when stacking). L2 only in most deployments — L3 routing lives at the distribution layer.

hack.memory: Access = store checkout lane. You scan ID (802.1X), bag limit per customer (port-security), security camera on rogue devices (BPDU guard), priority lane for VIPs (voice VLAN + QoS trust).
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, configure an access port: access VLAN 10, voice VLAN 20, PortFast, BPDU Guard, port-security max 2 sticky, and DHCP snooping trust = disabled (default). Verify with 'show run interface Gi0/1' and 'show port-security'.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17 (VLANs), Day 50 (security). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam guarantees questions on PortFast + BPDU Guard combo and port-security violation modes.

1.2.b.info:
The distribution layer is where Layer 2 meets Layer 3 in a three-tier campus. It aggregates access-switch uplinks and enforces all the routing, policy, and redundancy that the access layer doesn't touch. It is the policy layer of the network.

Distribution-layer responsibilities:

  • Inter-VLAN routing via SVIs or routed ports. This is where a packet crosses from one VLAN to another.
  • ACLs for traffic filtering between VLANs and toward the core/WAN.
  • QoS policy enforcement — classify, mark, queue, and shape traffic.
  • Route summarization toward the core — advertise a single summary route (e.g., 10.1.0.0/16) instead of every /24 behind the distribution block.
  • First-hop redundancy — HSRP/VRRP virtual IPs that endpoints use as default gateway.
  • STP root bridge placement — distribution switches are the root for all VLANs in their block.
  • Fast convergence — tuned with Rapid PVST+ or MST, sub-second failover.

Deployed as pairs: two distribution switches per block, connected to each other and to every access switch below. Each access switch is dual-homed.

Connection to core: typically routed links (L3 point-to-point, no switchport) running OSPF or EIGRP. This keeps the core pure L3 and prevents STP from extending into the core.

Hardware: Catalyst 9300, 9400, 9500 — L3 switches with hardware routing and robust ACL/QoS TCAM.

hack.memory: Distribution = airport security checkpoint. Bags (packets) get scanned (ACL), flagged (QoS), routed to the correct terminal (VLAN), and you get an escort if needed (HSRP). Core is the runway — no scanning there.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer three-tier: 2 dist switches with SVIs for 3 VLANs, HSRP between them, OSPF up to 2 core switches over routed /30 links. Verify inter-VLAN ping, HSRP active/standby, and OSPF neighbors.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 17-18 (inter-VLAN routing), Day 24 (architecture), Day 42 (FHRP). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17. Exam favorites: 'which layer does ACL/QoS/inter-VLAN?' = distribution.

1.2.b.info:
The core layer is the high-speed backbone that interconnects distribution blocks. Its job is one thing and one thing only: forward packets as fast as possible. Anything that adds processing — ACLs, QoS remarking, policy, complex route filtering — belongs in the distribution layer, not here.

Core design principles (memorize):

  • No policy — no ACLs, no QoS remarking, no NAT, no traffic filtering. Pure transit.
  • High bandwidth — 10G, 40G, 100G, 400G links. Uplinks typically match or exceed aggregate distribution bandwidth.
  • Low latency, predictable forwarding — hardware forwarding only, no CPU path.
  • Full redundancy — core deployed as a pair (or more), equal-cost paths, sub-second failover.
  • L3 routed — point-to-point routed links to distribution, running OSPF or EIGRP. No L2 trunks, no STP in the core.
  • Simple — fewer features enabled = fewer bugs, faster boot, less downtime.

Hardware: Catalyst 9500, 9600, or Nexus — high-port-density, high-throughput L3 switches.

Why no policy at core? Three reasons: (1) performance — policy lookups slow down forwarding. (2) scalability — as the network grows, core-layer policy becomes a single giant config that no one can safely edit. (3) reliability — every feature you enable in the core is a potential failure point in the part of the network that can't go down.

Classic exam trap: 'At which layer should ACLs be configured?' → distribution, never core.

hack.memory: Core = highway. Distribution = on-ramps with tolls and weigh stations. Access = your driveway. You would never put a toll booth in the middle of a highway — traffic would pile up. Same reason: no policy at the core.
hack.practice: Draw a three-tier topology. Label every layer's responsibilities. Mark every feature that is FORBIDDEN at the core with a red X: ACL, QoS remarking, NAT, inter-VLAN routing for user VLANs, HSRP for user gateways. This drill cements the 'core does nothing but forward' principle.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: 'Which layer should NOT enforce policy?' = core. This is a guaranteed point if you memorize the rule.

1.2.b.info:
ADC = Access → Distribution → Core. The three layers of the Cisco hierarchical campus model, in order from the edge (where users plug in) to the backbone (where high-speed transit happens). Memorizing ADC and each layer's role gives you instant answers to the majority of Domain 1.2 questions.

Per-layer cheat sheet:

  • A — Access: L2, endpoint connectivity, VLAN assignment, PoE, 802.1X, port security, PortFast/BPDU Guard, DHCP snooping, QoS trust boundary.
  • D — Distribution: L3, inter-VLAN routing, ACLs, QoS enforcement, HSRP/VRRP, STP root, route summarization.
  • C — Core: L3, fast transit only, no policy, high-bandwidth redundant links, ECMP.

Common exam phrasing:

  • 'Which layer performs inter-VLAN routing?' → Distribution.
  • 'Which layer should NOT perform ACL filtering?' → Core.
  • 'Which layer enforces port security?' → Access.
  • 'Which layer provides HSRP to end users?' → Distribution.
  • 'Which layer aggregates access switches?' → Distribution.
  • 'Which layer interconnects distribution blocks?' → Core.

Rule of thumb: policy + routing = distribution. Users + security = access. Speed + simplicity = core. If you remember those three pairings, ADC questions are free points.

hack.memory: ADC = 'A Defined Campus.' Access-Distribution-Core, bottom to top. Or reverse: CDA = 'Core Does Absolutely nothing but forward.'
hack.practice: Flashcard drill: write 10 exam-style 'which layer does X?' questions on one side, answer on the other. Shuffle and run through until you answer each one in under 2 seconds.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: ADC-based questions are guaranteed. This is the highest ROI memorization in Domain 1.2.

1.2.b.info:
Route summarization (also called route aggregation or supernetting) is the practice of advertising a single, less-specific route that represents many more-specific routes. In a three-tier design, distribution switches summarize routes toward the core so the core's routing table stays small and its convergence stays fast.

Example: a distribution block contains four /24 subnets:

  • 10.1.0.0/24
  • 10.1.1.0/24
  • 10.1.2.0/24
  • 10.1.3.0/24

Instead of advertising all four to the core, the distribution switch advertises a single 10.1.0.0/22 summary that covers all of them. The core installs one route instead of four. Multiply this across every distribution block and you save hundreds or thousands of routing table entries.

Benefits:

  • Smaller routing tables → faster lookups, less memory, faster convergence.
  • Fewer routing updates propagate across the network → less protocol chatter.
  • Failure hiding — if one /24 inside the block flaps, the summary stays stable and the rest of the network doesn't know anything changed.

Requires contiguous address blocks. You can only summarize nicely when the subnets in a block share a common prefix. This is why IP addressing plans for large networks allocate blocks hierarchically — each building/block gets a contiguous range.

Configuration (OSPF example):

router ospf 1
 area 1 range 10.1.0.0 255.255.252.0

This summarizes area 1's /24s into a single /22 advertised out of that area.

hack.memory: Route summarization = giving your boss the summary instead of 47 individual emails. Core says 'I know how to reach 10.1.0.0/22'; the distribution block handles the details of which /24 the traffic lands in. Boss is happy, emails are fewer, meetings are shorter.
hack.practice: Paper drill: given four subnets 172.16.8.0/24, 172.16.9.0/24, 172.16.10.0/24, 172.16.11.0/24 — what is the smallest summary? Convert each third octet to binary, find common prefix bits = 22 bits, answer 172.16.8.0/22. Do 5 of these until summarization math is instant.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 27 (OSPF area summarization). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 20. Exam: expect a 'given 4 subnets, pick the summary' multiple-choice question. Pure subnetting skill.

1.2.b.info:
Dual-homing means every access switch connects to both distribution switches via separate physical uplinks. This is the bedrock of three-tier (and two-tier) redundancy: a single distribution switch, link, or transceiver can fail without taking the access switch offline.

Two variants:

  • STP-based dual-homing — access switch has two uplinks, both in the same VLAN, both trunks. STP elects one as forwarding and blocks the other. On failure of the forwarding uplink, STP converges to the blocked path. Rapid PVST+ converges in ~1-6 seconds.
  • EtherChannel / MEC dual-homing — bundle the two uplinks into a LAG (LACP or static). Both links actively forward. Requires either stacking (StackWise, Catalyst 9300) or VSS/vPC (virtualized chassis across two distribution switches) so the bundle looks like it terminates on one logical switch.

Why two links aren't enough: you also need to think about control-plane redundancy — HSRP/VRRP at the distribution layer so the gateway IP survives, and STP root redundancy (primary + secondary root).

Classic failures handled:

  • Distribution switch reboots → access traffic flows via the other uplink.
  • Optic/transceiver dies on one uplink → STP or LACP reroutes to the survivor.
  • Cable cut → same as above.
  • Full distribution switch failure → HSRP fails over, access keeps routing.

Anti-pattern: single-uplink access switches. Loss of that one uplink or the upstream distribution switch takes down every user on that access switch. Only acceptable for truly non-critical environments.

hack.memory: Dual-homing = two ropes holding the climber. If one snaps, the other still holds. Single-homing = one rope. Cheap, but you're one fray away from falling off the network.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: access switch with two uplinks to two distribution switches. Configure both as trunks. Observe STP blocks one. Shut the forwarding uplink — watch the blocked uplink come up (takes ~1s on RPVST). Restore — watch it go back.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 19 (STP). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9-10. Exam: know that dual-homing + STP is a common pattern; know that EtherChannel across two physical switches requires stacking or VSS.

1.2.b.info:
Three-tier scales by adding more distribution blocks, not by making existing blocks bigger. This is the architectural key to the model and the reason large enterprise networks still use it despite spine-leaf taking over the data center.

How it scales:

  • Each distribution block is a self-contained unit: a pair of distribution switches + the access switches they aggregate. Typical size: 10-30 access switches per block, supporting ~500-1500 users.
  • When you outgrow a block, you add another block. Both blocks connect to the same core, which already had the capacity to absorb more distribution peers.
  • The core doesn't need to be replaced — it just gets another pair of routed links from the new block.
  • Route summarization at each block's distribution layer keeps the core's routing table stable regardless of how many blocks exist.

Real-world sizing: a campus with 5,000 users might have 4 distribution blocks (one per building) all connecting to a 2-switch core. A 50,000-user network might have dozens of blocks and a 4- or 6-switch core.

Failure isolation: a failure inside a distribution block doesn't affect other blocks. The core sees only the summary route disappear; everything else keeps running.

When three-tier starts to strain: when east-west traffic between servers in a data center dominates. Three-tier was designed for north-south campus traffic. For DC workloads (VM-to-VM, storage replication, microservices), spine-leaf has replaced three-tier because every leaf-to-leaf path is exactly two hops.

children.name: Dist Block N → access switches (add as needed)

hack.memory: Three-tier scales like a city. Each neighborhood (distribution block) has its own local streets (access). Highways (core) connect neighborhoods. Growing the city = add more neighborhoods, not wider streets.
hack.practice: Draw a three-tier with 2 distribution blocks. Then add a third block — note that only new routed links from block 3 to the core are needed; blocks 1 and 2 don't change. This drives home 'scale by adding blocks'.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: may ask why three-tier scales well — answer: modular distribution blocks + route summarization.
